zones.name: CLI End-to-End Tests
zones.description: Comprehensive end-to-end test suite that validates CLI argument contracts, delegation behavior, error handling, and architecture policy across the full monorepo orchestration layer.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion (1.0) with zero coupling indicates a well-isolated test suite that makes no cross-package assumptions — an ideal property for end-to-end tests.
The presence of architecture-policy.test.js alongside CLI tests elevates this zone beyond functional coverage into structural correctness enforcement, which is a strong design choice.
cli-contract.test.mjs using .mjs while all other files use .js is a minor inconsistency that could cause confusion about module format expectations in the test runner config.
architecture-policy.test.js colocated with CLI e2e tests is excellent — policy checks run in the same harness as behavioral tests, making violations impossible to miss.
cli-contract.test.mjs is the only .mjs file among .js peers — standardize to one extension to avoid potential vitest/jest config edge cases with module resolution.
19 files with cohesion 1.0 suggests a disciplined, focused test zone; continue keeping unit and integration tests in their own zones rather than mixing them here.

cli-e2e-tests zone tests the orchestration tier (cli.js, web.js) as a black box — this means regressions inside domain packages (rex, sourcevision, hench) are only observable here if they surface through the CLI contract, not if they are internal behavioral changes.

The e2e test zone sits at the monorepo root rather than inside any package, giving it a natural position to enforce cross-package contracts that no individual package test suite can enforce — this structural placement is deliberate and correct.

cli-e2e-tests validates CLI delegation but has no direct coverage of the MCP transport layer (HTTP session management, Streamable HTTP protocol) — MCP regressions could pass all e2e tests yet break Claude Code integration.
Zero coupling from cli-e2e-tests to any source zone means the test suite cannot be broken by internal refactors that preserve the CLI contract — this is ideal test isolation and should be maintained.

architecture-policy.test.js colocated in the CLI e2e zone likely checks the same gateway constraints as check-gateway-*.mjs via a different mechanism — if both exist without explicit deduplication, a gateway rule change must be updated in two places, risking drift between the script-based and test-based enforcement definitions.

The 19-file e2e zone mixes two qualitatively different test concerns: behavioral CLI contract tests (cli-contract, cli-delegation, cli-errors, etc.) and structural policy tests (architecture-policy) — these have different failure modes, different audiences, and different cadences, and would benefit from being in separate subdirectories even if they remain in the same zone.

Zero coupling from this zone means architecture-policy.test.js can only inspect architecture via filesystem reads or spawned CLI introspection — it cannot assert TypeScript module graph constraints directly, leaving compile-time import violations undetectable by this test.

architecture-policy.test.js and check-gateway-*.mjs in monorepo-maintenance-scripts likely enforce overlapping gateway constraints via different mechanisms (test harness vs. raw script) — without explicit documentation of which rules live where, the two enforcement layers will drift when rules are updated.

cli-contract.test.mjs uses .mjs while all 18 peer files use .js — module format inconsistency in a vitest suite can cause silent resolution differences if the runner applies different transform pipelines to .mjs vs .js files; standardize to .js.

The 19-file e2e zone contains no test file named after the 'usage' or 'sync' commands despite both being documented ndx commands (ndx usage, ndx sync) — either these commands are covered indirectly by cli-delegation.test.js or they have no e2e coverage, a distinction that is invisible from file names alone.

All e2e test files use flat naming (cli-*.test.js) with no subdirectory grouping despite covering qualitatively distinct concerns: behavioral (cli-errors, cli-delegation), contractual (cli-contract), and structural (architecture-policy) — as the file count grows past 19, the flat layout will make it harder to selectively run one category in CI.

The 'ndx usage' and 'ndx sync' commands have no obviously dedicated e2e test file. Confirm coverage exists in cli-delegation.test.js or add cli-usage.test.js and cli-sync.test.js to make coverage intent explicit and discoverable by file name.

19 flat files mixing behavioral, contractual, and structural test concerns will become hard to selectively execute in CI as the suite grows. Introduce subdirectories (tests/e2e/behavioral/, tests/e2e/structural/) to group files by concern and enable targeted test runs without changing the zone boundary.

zones.name: Hench Agent
zones.description: The autonomous agent package that picks rex tasks, builds execution briefs, drives Claude API tool-use loops, and records run transcripts.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
157 files with cohesion 1.0 and coupling 0 indicate a highly self-contained package with rich internal structure and no detectable cross-zone import leakage at the algorithmic level.

The agent/analysis/ subdirectory (adaptive.ts, review.ts, spin.ts, stuck.ts) suggests a multi-strategy loop controller — a clean design for handling diverse task states without a monolithic run function.

Zero coupling at the zone level is consistent with the gateway pattern: hench imports from rex only through src/prd/rex-gateway.ts, so all cross-package edges are concentrated in one file rather than distributed across the zone.
Cohesion of 1.0 across 157 files is excellent and reflects a well-bounded package with strong internal import density — the hench package is architecturally self-contained.

The rex-gateway.ts pattern concentrates all cross-package imports into a single file, which is the correct implementation of the gateway convention defined in CLAUDE.md — this makes the dependency surface auditable.

With 157 files, hench is the largest single-package zone; if internal cohesion starts to fragment as the package grows, consider whether agent loop logic and tool dispatch warrant sub-zone decomposition.
Zone "hench-agent" has files across 32 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Hench achieves zero zone-level coupling because the rex-gateway.ts concentrates all cross-package imports — but this means the community detector sees hench as self-contained while it has deep runtime coupling to rex-runtime-state via file I/O that is architecturally significant.
Hench is the only zone that bridges the execution tier to the rex-runtime-state data zone at runtime — making it the single point of failure for PRD mutation correctness.

Hench's zero coupling metric is a measurement artifact of the gateway pattern: all imports from rex pass through rex-gateway.ts, collapsing what would be a distributed coupling into a single file. This is architecturally correct but means zone coupling metrics underreport hench's true dependency surface — tooling should be aware of this gateway-induced blind spot.

Hench is the sole writer to .rex/execution-log.jsonl and a co-writer to .rex/prd.json alongside rex itself — two independent writers sharing a mutable JSON file without a locking protocol is a latent data-corruption risk if hench and rex CLI commands are ever run concurrently (e.g. in CI).

rex-gateway.ts imports 8 symbols from rex's public.ts at build time, but hench is built and distributed independently. If rex is updated and hench is not rebuilt, the stale hench dist/ will silently use the old gateway — there is no runtime version-compatibility assertion between hench and the rex package it imports.

Hench writes to .rex/execution-log.jsonl but no zone in the monorepo reads this file except for human inspection — if hench log format changes, no consumer breaks, making the log format a maintenance liability that can drift indefinitely without detection.

Hench and rex are independently buildable packages with no runtime version assertion. A mismatch between hench dist/ and installed rex version will produce a silent behavioral failure rather than a clear compatibility error at startup.

The hench analysis/ subdirectory forms a coherent agent-health diagnostic feedback loop rather than a naming inconsistency: stuck.ts detects hard failures (failed/timeout/budget_exceeded status codes), spin.ts detects token-wasting empty turns (many turns, zero tool calls), review.ts gates a manual approval checkpoint with git diff inspection, and adaptive.ts auto-scales workflow config based on velocity and token-burn trends. The naming convention is uniformly agent-state descriptive across all four files — the apparent asymmetry between adjectives (adaptive) and past-participles (stuck) reflects distinct health dimensions, not mixed conventions.

insights: High coupling (0.6) — 3 imports target "web-dashboard"
Coupling of 0.6 is at the warning threshold for a 3-file zone — most coupling comes from the bi-directional relationship with the Web Dashboard zone.
The domain-gateway.ts gateway file correctly concentrates all sourcevision cross-package imports in one place, following the project's gateway pattern.
Co-locating the MCP route test with production files creates a small but well-tested surface for a critical integration boundary.

Coupling of 0.6 is elevated for a 3-file zone; the mutual import relationship with web-dashboard (each zone imports from the other) is the primary driver — extracting shared types into a dedicated types module would reduce coupling on both sides.
The domain-gateway.ts file correctly follows the single-gateway-per-source-package pattern, making the sourcevision dependency surface explicit and auditable.
The MCP route handler test is the only test in this zone — verify that session management, error handling, and transport edge cases are covered given this is a critical external API boundary.

routes-mcp.ts is consumed by web-dashboard (start.ts imports handleMcpRoute) while itself importing from domain-gateway.ts — this creates a clean adapter pattern: mcp-route-layer translates between the MCP protocol boundary and the sourcevision domain, with web-dashboard acting as the composition root that wires them together.

The routes-rex.ts file in web-dashboard references the rex gateway pattern in comments but does NOT live in mcp-route-layer, meaning rex MCP routing is currently handled inside web-dashboard rather than through the dedicated MCP route zone — this inconsistency means sourcevision and rex MCP routes are architecturally split across two zones.

mcp-route-layer cleanly implements the Adapter pattern: domain-gateway.ts translates sourcevision imports, routes-mcp.ts translates MCP protocol concerns — the zone has a single well-defined responsibility with no logic leakage.

Rex MCP routing (routes-rex.ts) lives inside web-dashboard rather than mcp-route-layer, splitting MCP protocol handling across two zones — moving routes-rex.ts into mcp-route-layer would unify the MCP boundary and reduce web-dashboard's surface area.

Neither rex-gateway.ts nor domain-gateway.ts has a dedicated unit test — both gateway contracts are exercised only implicitly through the route handler tests that consume them. A misconfigured re-export (missing symbol, wrong alias) would surface at runtime rather than at test time.

domain-gateway.ts exports exactly one symbol (createSourcevisionMcpServer) while rex-gateway.ts exports 35+ symbols — this asymmetry means the two gateways have fundamentally different scopes, making the 'one gateway per source package' pattern appear consistent while masking a large design difference in surface area.

domain-gateway.ts has no dedicated test. As the sole web→sourcevision import seam, a silent re-export breakage (e.g. sourcevision renames createSourcevisionMcpServer) would only be caught when a route request reaches the MCP handler at runtime — not during build or test. A minimal gateway contract test (import the symbol, assert it is a function) would catch this at CI time.

The zone name 'mcp-route-layer' uses a '-layer' suffix that no other zone in the web package uses — all other zones use noun phrases ('web-dashboard', 'task-usage-analytics', 'landing-page', 'viewer-static-assets'). This naming inconsistency makes the zone appear to be a different category of abstraction when it is structurally equivalent to the others.

routes-mcp.ts contains @see JSDoc references to both gateways (rex-gateway.ts, domain-gateway.ts) but the test file (routes-mcp.test.ts) only validates the route handler behavior — if both gateway symbols are silently broken (e.g. renamed), the test file provides no signal because it presumably mocks the gateway imports. The documentation accuracy of the @see references is untested.

Rename zone 'mcp-route-layer' to 'mcp-routes' to match the noun-phrase naming convention used by all other web package zones ('web-dashboard', 'task-usage-analytics', 'landing-page'). The '-layer' suffix is an anomaly with no architectural distinction from the other zones.

The zone has coupling 0.6 with only 3 files, meaning over half of its inter-file edges cross the zone boundary into web-dashboard. At this coupling density, any refactor of the two files it imports from web-dashboard (start.ts or rex-gateway.ts) has a direct probability of breaking this zone — add a coupling threshold alert or zone-level import test to make this fragility visible before refactors.

Maintenance scripts use .mjs (untyped) while the entire source base is TypeScript — structural validation scripts that enforce TypeScript architectural rules are themselves exempt from TypeScript's type checking, creating an ironic enforcement gap where the enforcer cannot be enforced.

Zero coupling confirms this zone is a true root with no upstream dependencies.
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm the orchestration layer is correctly isolated — all cross-package coordination happens via spawned CLIs rather than library imports.

Orchestration scripts in this zone are the only code path that wires together rex, sourcevision, and hench at runtime — if any cross-package CLI contract changes (flag names, exit codes, stdout format), breakage surfaces here first, not in types.

The orchestration zone has no import edges to any other zone, meaning integration contract violations (e.g. changed CLI flags or output formats in rex/sourcevision/hench) will only manifest at runtime, not at build or typecheck time — consider adding a contract-test layer in e2e that validates CLI I/O contracts across package boundaries.

build.js (web package) and the root orchestration scripts (cli.js, web.js, ci.js) are all plain JavaScript in a TypeScript monorepo — type-checked API contracts from rex/sourcevision/hench cannot be enforced in these entry points, so breaking changes in package APIs silently pass typecheck until runtime.

The gateway-enforcement scripts (check-gateway-*.mjs in scripts/) operate by regex/filesystem inspection rather than import resolution, meaning they cannot detect a gateway file that correctly re-exports but also adds logic — violating the 're-export only' rule in CLAUDE.md without tripping the enforcement check.

Root orchestration scripts (cli.js, web.js, ci.js) are plain JS in a TypeScript monorepo, making cross-package API breakage undetectable at typecheck time. Even a full pnpm typecheck pass will not catch broken orchestration contracts.

config.js breaks the orchestration tier's spawn-only rule in a subtle way: unlike ci.js/web.js/cli.js which coordinate via spawned subprocesses, config.js directly implements config-merging logic (deepMerge, validators, loadJSON, saveJSON) and reads/writes .rex/config.json, .hench/config.json, .sourcevision/manifest.json, and .n-dx.json via async file I/O. It is an orchestration-tier file that encapsulates library-tier concerns, making it the only undocumented exception to the spawn-only rule.

CODEX.md exists at root alongside CLAUDE.md and mirrors the same documentation structure but adds a Codex-specific troubleshooting section (malformed output fallback, missing usage field mapping, token mismatch diagnostics). This establishes an implicit multi-vendor AI assistant convention — one instruction file per AI vendor — that is not documented as a policy anywhere, meaning future vendor additions have no template to follow.

web.js is documented in CLAUDE.md as a 'legacy name' alias for ndx start, but the file itself contains no @deprecated annotation, no runtime console.warn, and no redirect comment pointing to cli.js. Contributors discovering web.js without reading CLAUDE.md will treat it as a primary entry point rather than a legacy alias.

config.js implements config-merging logic (deepMerge, loadJSON, saveJSON, validators) and directly reads/writes package config files rather than delegating to a spawned CLI, violating the orchestration tier's documented spawn-only rule. This is the only root orchestration script that breaks this invariant. Either extract a dedicated config subcommand in a domain package, or explicitly document config.js as a spawn-exempt exception in CLAUDE.md.

web.js is a legacy alias with no deprecation marker in the file itself. Add a JSDoc @deprecated comment and a runtime console.warn to guide contributors toward ndx start before the alias is eventually removed.

CODEX.md mirrors CLAUDE.md but with Codex-specific content — this multi-vendor instruction-file convention is established by practice but not by policy. A brief policy note in CONTRIBUTING.md describing the expected structure and naming for per-vendor AI instruction files would prevent inconsistently structured files as more vendors are supported.

llm-types.ts is generically named in a zone scoped to provider concerns — if this file contains package-wide shared types (token structs, config shapes) rather than provider-only types, its placement in provider-core is the structural root cause of the 3 reverse imports: core holds types that utilities need, so utilities import core to get them rather than importing a neutral types zone.

Code inspection confirms api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts, auth.ts, create-client.ts, and llm-client.ts all import from types.ts, llm-types.ts, provider-interface.ts, and config.ts. If those type/config files are classified into the src-2 zone by Louvain clustering, these are the edges producing the bidirectional coupling count. Audit the 12 files in the src zone that have incoming import edges from src-2. Cross-reference each against src/public.ts exports to determine whether the import is correctly surfaced through the package entrypoint or bypasses it. Priority files to check: types.ts, llm-types.ts, provider-interface.ts, and config.ts — all confirmed as direct imports from api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts, auth.ts, and create-client.ts. If these type/config files are the source of all 12 entry points, the finding reflects zone boundary placement rather than an API surface violation.

src/0 (12 entry points, call-graph-derived) and global/2 (single entry-point pattern, LLM-inferred) describe different scopes: global/2 observes the public.ts convention's intent at the package boundary; src/0 counts intra-package cross-zone import edges, which are expected within a single package. Both can be simultaneously correct — the contradiction is terminological. global/2 should not be relied upon to disprove src/0.

Before treating the 12 entry-point count as a public API violation, confirm whether the edges are intra-package (src-2 files importing from src files within the same package) or cross-package (external consumers bypassing public.ts). Intra-package cross-zone imports are architectural zone concerns, not public API violations, and do not require remediation via public.ts consolidation. Only cross-package direct imports need to be routed through public.ts.

The web package's five sub-zones reveal a clear layered topology: viewer-static-assets and landing-page are terminal leaf nodes → task-usage-analytics is a domain service layer → mcp-route-layer is a protocol adapter → web-dashboard is the composition root. This emergent layer ordering was not explicitly designed but is structurally enforced by the import graph.

The only missing element to make the web package's architecture fully explicit is a consistent facade module pattern: task-usage-analytics has one (task-usage.ts), but mcp-route-layer exposes routes-mcp.ts directly without a facade — standardizing on facade modules at each zone boundary would make the inter-zone API surface self-documenting.

The rex MCP surface (routes-rex.ts) is absorbed into web-dashboard while the sourcevision MCP surface (routes-mcp.ts) lives in mcp-route-layer — this asymmetry means the MCP protocol boundary is not a single architectural zone but is split, making it harder to reason about the full external API surface of the server.

The two enforcement zones (monorepo-maintenance-scripts and cli-e2e-tests) have complementary blind spots: scripts catch static structural violations (gateway regex) but miss behavioral regressions; e2e tests catch behavioral CLI regressions but miss structural gateway drift — neither zone covers the other's gap, so both must run in CI.

The usage zone (5 files, cohesion 0.75) is imported only by web-viewer (3 imports) and has no reverse dependency — this is a clean unidirectional relationship and the usage zone is correctly positioned as a utility below the viewer layer.

The web-server and web-viewer bidirectional cycle (already flagged as critical) is not tested by any zone's contract — cli-e2e-tests exercises the server indirectly through CLI commands but does not assert zone-level import direction, leaving the cycle structurally invisible to automated enforcement.

monorepo-maintenance-scripts and cli-e2e-tests together achieve full enforcement coverage only when run in sequence (scripts first as a fast gate, e2e second as a deep gate) — if either is skipped or their order reversed in CI, fast-failing structural checks lose their value as a short-circuit.

The monorepo has two complementary enforcement strategies that operate at different levels: import-graph gateway files (rex-gateway.ts, domain-gateway.ts) enforce cross-package coupling at compile time, while maintenance scripts (check-gateway-*.mjs) enforce it structurally at runtime — both are necessary because gateways can be bypassed by adding imports outside the gateway file, which only the scripts catch.

Four zones achieve zero coupling (monorepo-root, hench-agent, rex-runtime-state, project-documentation, e2e) — but for different reasons: monorepo-root by design (spawn-only), hench-agent by gateway concentration, rex-runtime-state and project-documentation by file type (no JS imports), e2e by test strategy (CLI spawn). Conflating these distinct zero-coupling causes would lead to incorrect architectural conclusions.

The web package exhibits an emergent four-layer topology (static assets → domain services → protocol adapters → composition root) that mirrors the monorepo's four-tier hierarchy — this structural consistency across scales is a strong architectural signal that the decomposition is correct.

All three enforcement mechanisms (maintenance scripts, e2e tests, architecture-policy test) are zero-coupling consumers — they observe but never modify shared state, which means CI can safely parallelize them once the build step completes.

The web-viewer zone imports from both web-server (2 edges) and usage (3 edges) while web-server imports back from web-viewer (3 edges) — this bidirectional cycle between web-viewer and web-server, combined with the unidirectional web-viewer → usage, means web-viewer has the highest inter-zone coupling in the web package and is the most likely source of future architectural fragility.

Rex MCP routing (routes-rex.ts) sits inside web-dashboard while sourcevision MCP routing lives in the dedicated mcp-route-layer — consolidating both into mcp-route-layer would give the MCP boundary a single, auditable zone and shrink web-dashboard's responsibilities.

No zone currently enforces the four-tier dependency hierarchy at the import level for the rex and sourcevision packages — gateway scripts cover hench and web but the domain tier has no equivalent structural guard, creating an asymmetric enforcement posture.

The usage analytics zone (web-viewer → usage: 3 imports) establishes a clean downward dependency from viewer to utility, consistent with the four-tier hierarchy; this relationship should be documented as an intended pattern to prevent future consumers from importing usage directly from a higher tier.

The usage zone (5 files, cohesion 0.75) is imported exclusively by web-viewer with no other consumers. A zone with a single consumer and no outward dependencies is a candidate for internalization — absorbing usage into web-viewer would eliminate the cross-zone edge without architectural loss and reduce the total zone count.

The web-landing zone (3 files, cohesion 0) contains only static assets with no import edges. At 3 files it is below the threshold where zone isolation provides navigational value, and it will always report zero cohesion — merging it into web-build-infrastructure would reduce noise in zone metrics without obscuring any meaningful boundary.

The web-server zone contains only 3 files yet sits at the center of the bidirectional cycle with web-viewer. Its small size relative to its structural importance suggests it is an incomplete abstraction: the server layer likely needs additional facade modules to give web-viewer a stable interface to depend on, which would also provide a natural seam to break the reverse dependency (web-server → web-viewer).

No zone in this monorepo has an explicit public interface test — the pattern is: write a gateway file, enforce gateway existence via a script, but never assert which symbols the gateway exports. If a gateway symbol is renamed or removed, downstream consumers fail at import resolution rather than at a dedicated contract test. Adding a gateway shape test per consumer package would catch this class of breakage in CI.

The four-tier hierarchy (orchestration → execution → domain → foundation) is documented and structurally enforced at the import level, but there is no tier-membership manifest that tooling can query — zone enrichment, gateway scripts, and CLAUDE.md all describe the tiers independently with no single source of truth. A machine-readable tier-membership file (e.g., in .sourcevision/tiers.json) would allow all three enforcement layers to reference one canonical definition.

The rex-gateway.ts surface (35+ re-exports) versus domain-gateway.ts surface (1 re-export) exposes a structural inconsistency: the two gateways enforce the same pattern at different granularities. If rex's public API is large enough to warrant 35 re-exports, a secondary 'web-rex-types-gateway' for type-only re-exports and a 'web-rex-ops-gateway' for runtime operations would separate the compile-time and runtime dependency surfaces, making it easier to audit which runtime operations the web layer actually needs.

No zone currently tests the gateway contract itself — all three gateways (rex-gateway.ts, domain-gateway.ts, hench/rex-gateway.ts) are validated only transitively through consumers. Adding a single gateway-contract test per gateway (assert all re-exported symbols exist and have the expected type) would create a fast-fail layer that catches upstream API renames before route-handler tests run.

The web package contains two types of route files: REST API routes (routes-rex.ts, routes-*.ts in web-dashboard) and MCP protocol routes (routes-mcp.ts in mcp-route-layer). These two categories have different consumers, different stability requirements, and different testing strategies, yet they share no zone boundary — consolidating MCP routes into mcp-route-layer would create a clean protocol-boundary zone separate from the REST API layer.

routes-rex.ts directly mutates the PRD file (readFileSync/writeFileSync on prd.json) rather than delegating to a rex domain function — this bypasses rex's own write path, creating a second untracked write channel to the shared .rex/prd.json state file. Any concurrency or schema-migration logic in rex's store module is silently skipped for operations that go through this route.

The monorepo has three enforcement layers (tsc, maintenance scripts, e2e tests) but they operate at disjoint levels: tsc catches TypeScript-level import violations, scripts catch filesystem-level gateway pattern drift, and e2e tests catch behavioral CLI regressions. No single layer covers all three surfaces simultaneously — a regression must land in the wrong layer's blind spot to go undetected. This three-layer gap map should be documented explicitly so future contributors know which layer to update when adding a new architectural constraint.

architecture-policy.test.js (in cli-e2e-tests) and check-gateway-*.mjs (in monorepo-maintenance-scripts) are potential rule-duplication points: both enforce structural constraints but via different mechanisms. Without a canonical source of truth for gateway rules (e.g. a shared config file consumed by both), the two layers will drift. This is a cross-zone coherence risk that currently has no mitigation.

The monorepo has zero zones that enforce the four-tier hierarchy at the TypeScript import-graph level for the rex and sourcevision domain packages — tsc enforces types, maintenance scripts enforce gateway file patterns, and e2e tests enforce CLI behavior, but none of these catch a direct import from hench into rex bypassing the gateway if the import resolves correctly. An eslint-plugin-import or custom tsc path-alias rule would close this gap.

The cli-e2e-tests zone (19 files, cohesion 1) and monorepo-maintenance-scripts zone (4 files, cohesion 0) together represent the full static enforcement surface of the monorepo — their combined zero-coupling property means enforcement is entirely read-only and observer-safe, but it also means neither zone can programmatically repair the violations it detects. All remediation is manual, which is acceptable today but becomes a scaling concern as the monorepo grows.

Gateway shape is enforced (file must exist, imports must pass through it) but gateway contract is not tested (exported symbol set is never asserted). A renamed or removed symbol in rex public.ts breaks hench/web at runtime but passes all static enforcement checks.

The usage zone has exactly one consumer (web-viewer) and no dependents of its own — single-consumer zones with no further fan-out represent premature abstraction boundaries that add zone-count complexity without enabling reuse.

web-landing (3 files, cohesion 0) is too small to produce meaningful zone metrics and will permanently report zero cohesion due to containing only static assets. Its presence inflates zone count and creates false-alarm metric noise.

routes-rex.ts contains direct filesystem writes to .rex/prd.json (readFileSync/writeFileSync) rather than delegating all mutations through the rex domain layer. This creates a second undocumented write channel to the shared PRD state, bypassing any locking, validation, or migration logic in rex's store module. If rex ever adds atomic writes or schema migrations to its store, these direct writes will silently diverge.

The two gateways in the web package (rex-gateway.ts: 35+ exports, domain-gateway.ts: 1 export) enforce the same structural pattern but at incompatible granularities — this makes it impossible to reason about the web package's cross-package dependency surface as a whole. A gateway audit would need to read two files of vastly different complexity to answer the question 'what does web depend on externally?'

No automated enforcement exists for the four-tier dependency hierarchy (Orchestration → Execution → Domain → Foundation) at the TypeScript import level for domain packages (rex, sourcevision). A developer can add a direct cross-domain import that tsc resolves correctly, bypasses the gateway pattern check (if the file path matches the gateway regex), and never surfaces in e2e tests — the violation would be structurally invisible across all three enforcement layers.

The boundary between monorepo-maintenance-scripts (structural checks) and cli-e2e-tests (behavioral + policy checks) is undocumented — contributors adding a new architectural rule have no guidance on whether to add it as a .mjs script, a vitest test in architecture-policy.test.js, or both. This ambiguity will produce inconsistent enforcement coverage over time.

packages/claude-client/ is a completely abandoned migration artifact: it contains only a /dist/ directory with TypeScript declaration files, no /src/ directory, no package.json, and zero consumers in the codebase — all packages import exclusively from @n-dx/llm-client. Its presence inflates package count and creates navigational confusion for contributors investigating the foundation tier.

The web rex-gateway.ts re-exports approximately 36 symbols while hench rex-gateway.ts re-exports approximately 8. At 36 re-exports, the web gateway approaches the full breadth of rex's public.ts barrel — meaning the gateway provides auditability but not encapsulation. A secondary split into a read-only query gateway and a mutation operations gateway would enforce least-privilege at the inter-package boundary and make it immediately visible which web operations modify PRD state.

The multi-vendor AI assistant instruction file convention (CLAUDE.md for Anthropic, CODEX.md for OpenAI/Codex) is established by practice but not documented as policy. The two files share structure but CODEX.md adds a vendor-specific troubleshooting section absent from CLAUDE.md. Without a policy document describing required sections, future vendor files (Gemini, Grok, etc.) will have inconsistent structure and coverage.

config.js is the only root orchestration script that encapsulates library-level logic rather than spawning a CLI, making it an undocumented exception to the four-tier hierarchy's spawn-only orchestration rule. This exception is invisible to contributors reading CLAUDE.md's orchestration description, which describes all root scripts as spawn-only coordinators.

The web package's 30-file server/ directory hosts files from three distinct zones (web-dashboard, mcp-route-layer, task-usage-analytics) with no filesystem organization to reflect zone membership — this is the only directory in the monorepo where zone boundaries are invisible to directory-level navigation, making it the highest-friction area for onboarding contributors.

aggregation-cache.ts uses only node:path and node:fs/promises with zero domain imports — it is a pure infrastructure utility with no coupling to rex, sourcevision, or any web-specific domain. This zero-domain-coupling property makes it a candidate for promotion to the @n-dx/llm-client foundation layer or a shared utilities package, removing it from the composition root entirely.

concurrent-execution-metrics.ts (383 lines, ring buffer, per-task resource tracking) and process-memory-tracker.ts together form an implicit 'server observability' subsystem inside web-dashboard that is never referenced as a cohesive unit in zone analysis. Both files measure runtime resource consumption but belong to no dedicated zone — if server observability becomes a cross-package concern (e.g. hench needs to report its own metrics), these files would need extraction with no existing zone boundary to guide the refactor.

The three routes-mcp.ts @see JSDoc references (to rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts) represent the only machine-readable cross-zone dependency documentation in the entire web package. All other cross-zone dependencies are discoverable only via import-graph analysis. Standardizing @see gateway annotations on all route files would make the cross-zone dependency surface self-documenting at the source level, not just at the analysis level.

Zone naming in the web package follows no enforced convention: 'web-dashboard' uses a compound adjective-noun, 'mcp-route-layer' uses acronym-noun-noun, 'task-usage-analytics' uses noun-noun-noun, 'landing-page' uses verb-noun, 'viewer-static-assets' uses noun-adjective-noun. A zone naming policy (e.g., '{domain}-{role}' where role is one of: core, routes, services, assets, gateway) would make zone identity parseable from the name alone.

The monorepo has no middle-tier enforcement layer between structural script checks (gateway file existence) and full behavioral e2e tests (CLI invocation). A fast unit-level gateway contract test — asserting that each gateway module exports exactly the expected symbol set — would fill this gap and fail in under a second, far faster than spawning e2e CLI processes.

hench-callgraph-analysis.mjs in monorepo-maintenance-scripts is the only file in the entire enforcement surface that is package-scoped rather than monorepo-scoped. This creates an implicit category error: the scripts zone is documented as a monorepo-wide enforcement zone, but one of its four files enforces a single-package concern. This asymmetry will compound if each package adds its own callgraph analysis script to the zone.

The cli-e2e-tests zone achieves perfect cohesion (1.0) by interconnecting all 19 test files, yet the enforcement scripts zone has zero cohesion (0) across 4 files — the two complementary enforcement layers have opposite internal structures, meaning they cannot share test utilities or helpers without one zone losing its cohesion property. This structural incompatibility is a hidden constraint on future enforcement tooling design.

Across all enforcement zones (monorepo-maintenance-scripts, cli-e2e-tests, architecture-policy.test.js), there is no test or script that validates the four-tier dependency direction for the rex and sourcevision domain packages specifically. The enforcement surface has a systematic blind spot at the domain tier: a direct import from hench into sourcevision (bypassing the gateway) would pass tsc, pass gateway regex checks, pass all e2e tests, and only be caught by a manual code review.

packages/claude-client/ contains no source files — only a stale /dist/ and /node_modules/ from a completed migration to @n-dx/llm-client, with zero consumers in the codebase. Remove the directory to eliminate false package-count inflation and contributor confusion about which foundation package is active.

Six web server route and index files directly read or write .rex/prd.json via readFileSync/writeFileSync, bypassing rex-gateway.ts entirely. This is the most critical gateway-pattern violation in the codebase: it creates six undocumented write channels to shared PRD state, each skipping rex's store-level locking and schema validation. Audit and route all web prd.json access through rex-gateway.ts.

The web rex-gateway.ts re-exports ~36 symbols, approaching the full surface of rex's public.ts. Splitting into a read-only query gateway and a write/mutation gateway would enforce least-privilege at the web→rex boundary, shrink each gateway's surface to an intentional subset, and make it immediately auditable which web operations mutate PRD state.

CLAUDE.md and CODEX.md establish a per-vendor AI instruction file convention by practice but no policy describes required file structure, naming, or mandatory sections. Add a policy note to CONTRIBUTING.md or README.md so future vendor instruction files follow a consistent template.

The packages/web/src/server/ directory contains 30 files from three zones with no subdirectory grouping. Apply a directory-per-zone convention by moving task-usage analytics files into server/usage/, mcp route files into server/mcp/, and infrastructure utilities (aggregation-cache, concurrent-execution-metrics, pr-markdown-refresh-diagnostics, process-memory-tracker) into server/infra/. This would make zone membership discoverable without running sourcevision analysis.

aggregation-cache.ts imports only node built-ins (node:path, node:fs/promises) and has zero domain coupling — it is misplaced in the web package composition root. Evaluate moving it to a shared utilities layer or @n-dx/llm-client foundation package, as its LRU-with-fingerprint-invalidation logic is generic enough to serve any package that needs filesystem-based caching.

Establish a zone naming policy for the web package using a '{domain}-{role}' pattern where role is one of: core, routes, services, assets, gateway. Apply retroactively: 'web-dashboard' → 'web-core', 'mcp-route-layer' → 'mcp-routes', 'task-usage-analytics' → 'usage-services'. This makes zone identity parseable from the name without requiring architectural context.

Add a gateway-contract.test.ts per consumer package (hench, web) that statically asserts the exported symbol set of each gateway file matches the expected interface. This fills the gap between 'gateway file exists' (scripts) and 'CLI behavior is correct' (e2e) with a fast, precise contract layer that catches upstream renames before any process is spawned.

The two enforcement zones (monorepo-maintenance-scripts, cli-e2e-tests) have no shared documentation of which architectural rules each owns. Add a single ENFORCEMENT.md at the monorepo root that maps each architectural constraint to its enforcement mechanism (tsc / maintenance script / architecture-policy test / e2e test), preventing future contributors from adding duplicate or conflicting enforcement for the same rule.

hench-callgraph-analysis.mjs is the only enforcement-surface file scoped to a single package (hench) rather than the full monorepo. Establish an explicit policy: scripts in scripts/ must be monorepo-scoped; package-scoped analysis scripts belong in packages/{name}/scripts/. Enforce this by adding a check to the CI pipeline that rejects package-name-prefixed scripts in scripts/.

These three files share no dependency on the composition root and represent caching, observability, and diagnostics concerns that are currently invisible inside a 370-file zone.

The current name actively misleads contributors about where server infrastructure, CLI entrypoint, and route dispatch logic live.
Cohesion of 0.75 with coupling of 0.25 is a healthy balance — the zone is internally connected without being overly dependent on the rest of the system.
The scheduler pattern (usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts) cleanly separates lifecycle management from domain logic — this is a good architectural separation for background maintenance operations.
The web-dashboard zone imports from this zone (3 imports) — ensure the public API surface of task-usage.ts is stable and minimal to avoid coupling the viewer to internal accumulation logic.
Zero coupling from cli-e2e-tests to any source zone means the test suite cannot be broken by internal refactors that preserve the CLI contract — this is ideal test isolation and should be maintained.

landing-page and viewer-static-assets form a matched pair of terminal zones (zero coupling, zero cohesion) — they can be versioned, replaced, or deleted independently without impacting any other zone.

mcp-route-layer cleanly implements the Adapter pattern: domain-gateway.ts translates sourcevision imports, routes-mcp.ts translates MCP protocol concerns — the zone has a single well-defined responsibility with no logic leakage.

Scripts zone is a pure leaf in the dependency graph (coupling 0, no imports from other zones) — this property should be preserved; any future shared utility pulled in from a package would create an implicit build-time dependency on that package.

Architecture docs (memory-architecture.md, level-system-reference.md) describe runtime behaviors and design patterns that have no mechanical link to the source zones they describe — consider adding a CI step (e.g. sourcevision validate) that flags when source structure diverges significantly from documented zone boundaries.

The .rex/ directory acts as an implicit inter-package message bus: rex writes proposals and PRD state, hench reads and updates task status, web serves it — treating this directory's schema as a formal versioned contract (similar to an API version) would reduce silent breakage risk.

task-usage-analytics is the only zone in the web package with a provably acyclic, unidirectional dependency relationship with its consumer — the explicit facade module (task-usage.ts) is the mechanism that enforces this clean boundary.

viewer-static-assets and landing-page are structurally identical terminal zones — if either grows to include TypeScript logic, the community detector will automatically migrate those files into the appropriate code zone on the next analysis run.

Once viewer files are reassigned, this zone becomes a zero-import config cluster similar to rex-runtime-state and project-documentation — consider explicitly tagging it as a build-config zone in sourcevision hints so cohesion/coupling metrics are interpreted correctly rather than triggering false architectural warnings.

cli-e2e-tests validates CLI delegation but has no direct coverage of the MCP transport layer (HTTP session management, Streamable HTTP protocol) — MCP regressions could pass all e2e tests yet break Claude Code integration.

Hench is the sole writer to .rex/execution-log.jsonl and a co-writer to .rex/prd.json alongside rex itself — two independent writers sharing a mutable JSON file without a locking protocol is a latent data-corruption risk if hench and rex CLI commands are ever run concurrently (e.g. in CI).

Hench's zero coupling metric is a measurement artifact of the gateway pattern: all imports from rex pass through rex-gateway.ts, collapsing what would be a distributed coupling into a single file. This is architecturally correct but means zone coupling metrics underreport hench's true dependency surface — tooling should be aware of this gateway-induced blind spot.

Rex MCP routing (routes-rex.ts) lives inside web-dashboard rather than mcp-route-layer, splitting MCP protocol handling across two zones — moving routes-rex.ts into mcp-route-layer would unify the MCP boundary and reduce web-dashboard's surface area.

monorepo-maintenance-scripts enforces gateway discipline for hench and web but has no equivalent check for the rex or sourcevision packages, leaving those gateways unguarded by static script validation.

The orchestration zone has no import edges to any other zone, meaning integration contract violations (e.g. changed CLI flags or output formats in rex/sourcevision/hench) will only manifest at runtime, not at build or typecheck time — consider adding a contract-test layer in e2e that validates CLI I/O contracts across package boundaries.

Rex-runtime-state is a shared mutable filesystem interface consumed by rex (writer), hench (reader/writer via rex-gateway), and web-server (reader via MCP) — this hidden fan-in dependency is invisible to import-graph tooling and creates a schema coupling risk across three tiers of the hierarchy.
web-dashboard is the hub node in the web package's hub-and-spoke topology — all other zones either import from it or export to it exclusively, with no zone-to-zone edges bypassing it.

architecture-policy.test.js and check-gateway-*.mjs in monorepo-maintenance-scripts likely enforce overlapping gateway constraints via different mechanisms (test harness vs. raw script) — without explicit documentation of which rules live where, the two enforcement layers will drift when rules are updated.

cli-contract.test.mjs uses .mjs while all 18 peer files use .js — module format inconsistency in a vitest suite can cause silent resolution differences if the runner applies different transform pipelines to .mjs vs .js files; standardize to .js.

Hench and rex are independently buildable packages with no runtime version assertion. A mismatch between hench dist/ and installed rex version will produce a silent behavioral failure rather than a clear compatibility error at startup.

domain-gateway.ts has no dedicated test. As the sole web→sourcevision import seam, a silent re-export breakage (e.g. sourcevision renames createSourcevisionMcpServer) would only be caught when a route request reaches the MCP handler at runtime — not during build or test. A minimal gateway contract test (import the symbol, assert it is a function) would catch this at CI time.

Maintenance scripts use .mjs (untyped) while the entire source base is TypeScript — structural validation scripts that enforce TypeScript architectural rules are themselves exempt from TypeScript's type checking, creating an ironic enforcement gap where the enforcer cannot be enforced.

No shared utility module exists across the 4 scripts; as the script count grows, ad-hoc duplication of filesystem traversal and exit-code patterns will accumulate. Extract a shared scripts/util.mjs when a third script reuses the same pattern.

Root orchestration scripts (cli.js, web.js, ci.js) are plain JS in a TypeScript monorepo, making cross-package API breakage undetectable at typecheck time. Even a full pnpm typecheck pass will not catch broken orchestration contracts.
pending-proposals.json is written by rex analyze and read by rex recommend with no file-level locking. Concurrent CLI invocations (common in CI) can produce a torn read/write on this file.

prd.json lacks a schema version field, making it impossible for consumers (hench, web) to detect format incompatibility at load time. A silent schema mismatch between package versions will corrupt PRD state without a diagnostic error.

esbuild configuration in build.js/dev.js is untyped JavaScript — invalid option names or value types silently fail at build time with no typecheck safety net. Migrating to tsx-executed TypeScript build scripts would close this gap.

rex-gateway.ts re-exports 35+ symbols covering domain types, constants, tree utilities, analytics, health, and reshape operations — it mirrors the entire rex public API rather than defining a minimal web-specific interface. A true gateway should expose only what consumers need, providing a stable seam; a full-mirror gateway offers no stability guarantee and generates false confidence that coupling is controlled.

routes-rex.ts is a 1000+ line file that handles route dispatch, input validation, tree mutations, prune logic, merge orchestration, analysis triggering, and requirements CRUD — it has at least 6 distinct responsibilities. This makes it the highest-risk file in the monorepo: a change to any one concern (e.g. prune logic) requires reading and testing the entire file.

19 flat files mixing behavioral, contractual, and structural test concerns will become hard to selectively execute in CI as the suite grows. Introduce subdirectories (tests/e2e/behavioral/, tests/e2e/structural/) to group files by concern and enable targeted test runs without changing the zone boundary.

The 'ndx usage' and 'ndx sync' commands have no obviously dedicated e2e test file. Confirm coverage exists in cli-delegation.test.js or add cli-usage.test.js and cli-sync.test.js to make coverage intent explicit and discoverable by file name.

Rename zone 'mcp-route-layer' to 'mcp-routes' to match the noun-phrase naming convention used by all other web package zones ('web-dashboard', 'task-usage-analytics', 'landing-page'). The '-layer' suffix is an anomaly with no architectural distinction from the other zones.

The zone has coupling 0.6 with only 3 files, meaning over half of its inter-file edges cross the zone boundary into web-dashboard. At this coupling density, any refactor of the two files it imports from web-dashboard (start.ts or rex-gateway.ts) has a direct probability of breaking this zone — add a coupling threshold alert or zone-level import test to make this fragility visible before refactors.

Naming inconsistency: three scripts use verb-object form ('check-gateway-regex', 'check-gateway-test', 'test-zone-consistency') but use two different verb prefixes ('check-' vs 'test-'). Standardize on one prefix (e.g. 'check-') to make the script inventory self-documenting and prevent the next script from introducing a third convention.

hench-callgraph-analysis.mjs is named after a specific package while check-gateway-*.mjs and test-zone-consistency.mjs are package-agnostic. Either rename it to reflect a cross-package concern (e.g. callgraph-analysis.mjs) or move it into packages/hench/ as a package-local dev script. The current placement conflates package-scoped introspection with monorepo-wide enforcement.
